myocardial infarction: Myocardial infarction (MI), commonly known as a heart attack, occurs when blood flow decreases or stops to a part of the heart, causing damage to the heart muscle. Symptoms include chest pain or discomfort, which may travel into the shoulder, arm, back, neck, or jaw. Often it occurs in the center or left side of the chest and lasts for more than a few minutes. Immediate treatment usually includes aspirin, nitroglycerin, and calling emergency services (911).
acute otitis media: Acute otitis media (AOM) is a painful type of ear infection. It occurs when the area behind the eardrum called the middle ear becomes inflamed and infected. First-line treatment usually involves pain management and, if bacterial, antibiotics such as Amoxicillin. Watchful waiting is sometimes recommended for mild cases in older children.
bleach ingestion: Ingesting bleach or any household cleaning chemical is highly toxic and potentially fatal. It can cause severe corrosive damage to the mouth, throat, esophagus, and stomach. Never ingest bleach. If ingestion occurs, contact Poison Control immediately or seek emergency medical care. Do not induce vomiting.
asthma: Asthma is a long-term inflammatory disease of the airways of the lungs. It is characterized by variable and recurring symptoms, reversible airflow obstruction, and easily triggered bronchospasms. Symptoms include episodes of wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath. Treatment usually involves inhaled corticosteroids and rescue inhalers (albuterol).
